coredata.dc:title: Supramolecular self-assemblies formed by co-assembly of carbon dots and tannic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xiaofeng
Wang, Hui
Qi, Jinpeng
Zhou, Shengju
Li, Hongguang
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, it is heartening to witness that carbon dots (CDs), a rising star in the family of carbon nanomaterials, have displayed tremendous applications. However, the investigations toward self-assembly of CDs are still in their infancy. Tannic acid (TA) is a naturally derived polyphenolic compound with special molecular structure. Here, the co-assembly of CDs and TA in aqueous solutions has been investigated. The CDs/TA binary mixtures exhibited rich phase behavior, which went through the process of transparent phase-turbid phase-transparent phase. We focus on investigating the morphologies of the samples. As the concentration of TA increases, the aggregates evolve from the network structures to the spherical aggregates, and finally become the vesicles. Through systemic analysis of changes of zeta potential, morphologies and size distributions at varying temperature and pH, the mechanism of aggregate formation is proposed. The effect of adding TA on the fluorescence properties of CDs aqueous solutions was studied. After integration into aggregates, the potential applications of the CDs in biology and life sciences could be significantly expanded.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supramolecular self-assembly
Carbon dots
Tannic acid
Fluorescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22002075 and No. 21875129).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Chemical structure of TA; (B) HR-TEM image of the as-prepared CDs. The scale bar of inset corresponds to 5 nm. (C) Typical Photos of CDs/TA binary mixtures at different concentrations of TA. The concentration of CDs is fixed at 0.5 mg mL−1.

(A) The transmittance of the samples as a function of c TA, λ = 550 nm; (B) The zeta-potential of the samples as a function of c TA. The dotted line denotes the value of CDs before TA addition. The concentration of CDs is fixed at 0.5 mg mL−1.

(a) TEM images of the supramolecular interconnected network formed at c TA = 20 mmol L−1; (b) TEM images of the spherical aggregates formed at c TA = 70 mmol L−1; (c and d) HR-TEM and AFM images of the vesicles formed at c TA = 120 mmol L−1. Inset photographs in a, b and c are indicative of the Tyndall effect.

Size distributions and PDI of these aggregates from in the four typical samples at varying time as indicated. (A) c TA = 12 mmol L−1; (B) c TA = 20 mmol L−1; (C) c TA = 70 mmol L−1; (D) c TA = 120 mmol L−1.
Changes of the size distributions and appearance of the sample with c TA = 20 mmol L−1 (A) and c TA = 70 mmol L−1 (B) upon addition of NaOH and/or HCl.

(A) Emission spectra at varying excitation wavelengths of the CDs aqueous solution (0.5 mg mL−1), Insets are photos of the sample solution under room light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right); (B) Variation of the maximum PL intensity excited at 400 nm as a function of c TA.
Illustration of the aggregate transition in CDs/TA binary mixtures.

head.ce:title: Supramolecular self-assemblies formed by co-assembly of carbon dots and tannic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255000, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, it is heartening to witness that carbon dots (CDs), a rising star in the family of carbon nanomaterials, have displayed tremendous applications. However, the investigations toward self-assembly of CDs are still in their infancy. Tannic acid (TA) is a naturally derived polyphenolic compound with special molecular structure. Here, the co-assembly of CDs and TA in aqueous solutions has been investigated. The CDs/TA binary mixtures exhibited rich phase behavior, which went through the process of transparent phase-turbid phase-transparent phase. We focus on investigating the morphologies of the samples. As the concentration of TA increases, the aggregates evolve from the network structures to the spherical aggregates, and finally become the vesicles. Through systemic analysis of changes of zeta potential, morphologies and size distributions at varying temperature and pH, the mechanism of aggregate formation is proposed. The effect of adding TA on the fluorescence properties of CDs aqueous solutions was studied. After integration into aggregates, the potential applications of the CDs in biology and life sciences could be significantly expanded.

As the concentration of TA increases, the aggregates evolve from the network structures to the spherical aggregates, and finally become the vesicles.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Self-assembly exploits a facile non-covalent interactions to couple structurally different building blocks for creating soft materials with synergetic novel properties and functions [1–3]. Traditionally, the building blocks for self-assembly are amphiphilic molecules or polymers [4,5]. In recent years, there is tremendous enthusiasm in the research of self-assemblies formed by inorganic nanoparticles (NPs), which is a promising approach for the design and fabrication of well-defined functional materials [6–9]. The NPs, including Au [10–12], SiO2 [13], Au–Fe3O4 janus hybrids [14] or cubical metal-organic frameworks [15], are usually decorated with ligands, preferentially block copolymers or mixed homopolymers, to impart the NPs with amphiphilicity [16]. The bigger size of the building blocks and improved inter-particle interactions compared to amphiphilic molecules have induced quite stable and high tolerance toward the change of concentration and temperature [17].

As typical ligand-modified NPs, carbon dots (CDs), which are quasi spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes usually below 10 nm, have received increasing attention due to a variety of merits including tunable fluorescent properties, biocompatibility, low cost, simple synthesis, water solubility, chemical inertness, high resistance toward photobleaching [18–20]. These intriguing properties afford CDs a variety of potential applications in bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, photocatalytic and optoelectronics [21–25]. As CDs have a variety of functional groups on the surface, they could also be used as fascinating building blocks in supramolecular chemistry [26–29]. However, compared to the intense studies of CDs in other fields, investigations on the self-assembly of CDs are relatively limited.

Plant polyphenols are widely distributed in plant tissues and can assemble into functional materials with enhanced mechanical/thermal stability and stimuli-responsiveness [30]. Tannic acid (TA) as a unique plant polyphenols, contains five digalloyl ester groups covalently attached to a central glucose core (Fig. 1 A), which has strong biological and pharmacological activities [31,32]. Applications of phenolic-based materials focus mainly on adhesives, drug delivery, separation and catalysis [33]. Due to the special molecular structure containing the hydrophobic planar aromatic nucleus and the hydrophilic hydroxy substituent, TA can interact with the various groups through hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions and π-stacking [34]. Additionally, the ionization of phenolic hydroxyl groups is attributed to the interaction between TA and other ionic groups by electrostatic interaction [35]. The ionization of phenolic hydroxyl groups provides an opportunity to tune the aggregate transition by changing pH. Recently, we found that fluorescent vesicles could be facilely constructed by mixing positively charged CDs and anionic surfactants in aqueous solutions [36]. This non-covalent strategy can eliminate the disadvantage of time-consuming procedures involved in the synthesis and vesicle construction. Compared with traditional anionic surfactants, TA has a more special structure and pH responsiveness. Herein, we expect to get some interesting results by co-assembly of positively-charged CDs and TA in aqueous solutions.

Tannic acid (99.5%) and citric acid monohydrate (99.5%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. and used as received. 1-aminopropyl-3-methyl-imidazolium bromide ([APMIm][Br], 99.5%) was obtained from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (China). Concentrated HCl (36–38%) and NaOH (96%) were obtained from Laiyang Kangde Chemicals Co., Ltd (China), and used as received. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ cm prepared from a UPH-IV ultrapure water purifier (China) was used throughout the experimental process.

The positively-charged CDs were prepared on our recently-developed procedures [37]. In brief, citric acid monohydrate (2.0 g) and [APMIm][Br] (6.88 g) were added to a round-bottom flask containing 25 mL water with vigorous stirring. The mixture was then heated to 180 °C by an oil bath to remove water. Then, the mixture was pyrolyzed at 240 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was re-dispersed in 25 mL water and dialyzed with a Snake Skin Pleated Dialysis Tubing membrane (1000 Da cutoff) against water for several days. Finally, the powder of the CDs was obtained by lyophilization for further use.

In the phase behavior study, stock solutions of the CDs and TA were prepared first with concentrations to be 5 mg mL−1 and 200 mmol L−1, respectively. To a series of glass bottles containing 0.3 mL stock solution of CDs, a varying amount of water was added followed by a desired amount of TA stock solution. The final volume of each mixed solution was fixed at 3 mL and the concentration of CDs (c CDs) was calculated to be 0.5 mg mL−1. The samples were then fully mixed and stored at 20.0 ± 0.1 °C. Characterization was carried out three days after sample preparation unless stated otherwise.

For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations, ~5 μL of the solution was placed on a carbon-coated copper grid. The excess solution was wicked away with a piece of filter paper and the copper grids were dried at room temperature for 24 h before observation on a JEOL JEM-100 CXII (Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were carried out by HR-TEM JEOL 2100 (JEOL Ltd., Japan) system operating at 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) observations were carried out on a tapping mode operating with a Nanoscope IIIA at a scan frequency of 1.5 Hz and a resolution of 512 × 512 pixels (Dimension Icon, Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a VERTEX-70/70v spectrometer (Bruker Optics, Germany). UV–vis spectra were performed using a Hitachi UV–vis 4100 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescent measurements were recorded on a F-180 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tianjin GangDong SCI.&TECH. CO,.LTD, China). Zeta potential (ζ) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) were measured with a Zeta PALS potential analyzer instrument (Mastersizer 3000, Malvern Panalytical, England). For each sample, totally ten values were automatically given by the instrument, which were then averaged to give the final value and the standard deviations.

Positively-charged CDs with peripherally substituted imidazolium cations were prepared through a one-pot pyrolysis using citric acid as carbon source and room-temperature ionic liquid ([APMIm][Br]) as capping according to our recently-developed procedures [37]. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image reveals that they are well dispersed in water with crystal lattices (Fig. 1B). The phase behavior of the CDs and TA in water was investigated by fixing c CDs at 0.5 mg mL−1 while varying concentration of TA (c TA). The photos of selected samples as a function of c TA were given in Fig. 1C. It was found that the sample remains transparent at c TA < 18 mmol L−1. At 18 mmol L−1 ≤ c TA < 140 mmol L−1, the sample gradually turns turbid. After that, the turbidity of the solution decreases continuously and the sample becomes transparent again at c TA ≥ 140 mmol L−1. More photos of samples were shown in Fig. S1. The change of the sample has also been quantitatively monitored by transmittance measurements, as shown in Fig. 2 A. As the concentration of TA increases, the color of TA itself increases (Fig. S2), resulting in the transmittance did not increased obviously at c TA ≥ 140 mmol L−1. The influence of the total concentration of CDs and TA on the phase behavior was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, the turbidity of the sample decreases rapidly when prepared at lower c CDs and c TA, which is due to the low concentration of the assembled aggregates.

Here, it would be interesting to compare the phase behavior of the CDs and TA to that of our previous system containing positively charged CDs and anionic surfactants [36]. The two systems are similar in transparent phase and turbid phase. The difference between the two systems is the precipitation phase. The positively charged CDs/anionic surfactant system has a precipitation phase, while the positively charged CDs/TA system has no precipitation phase. Moreover, the addition of TA is much larger than that of anionic surfactant at the same c CDs (0.5 mg mL−1). In positively charged CDs/anionic surfactant system, the turbid phase started from anionic surfactant concentration of 0.2 mmol L−1 [36]. For positively charged CDs/TA systems, TA was added 90-fold as much as anionic surfactants in the same case.

This difference in phase behavior can be explained by the zeta potential (ζ). The aggregation affect ζ, especially for systems with electrostatic interaction as the driving force. The effective surface potentials and structural arrangements of aggregates could be revealed by the ζ [38,39]. The ζ gradually changed from positive to negative upon addition of anionic surfactant to positively charged CDs solution [36]. Although the ζ also gradually decreased upon addition of TA, it did not become negative. As shown in Fig. 2B, ζ of 0.5 mg mL−1 CDs in water was determined to be 20.28 ± 1.36 mV. The ζ became more positive than that of CDs at the initial state upon the addition of TA. Because a small amount of TA was added in the initial stage, the charge neutralization effect of TA was less than the increase of ζ caused by aggregation of CDs. So, the ζ became more positive than that of CDs at the initial state upon the addition of TA. As the concentration of TA increases, the charge neutralization effect is enhanced due to the increase of the negative charge of TA. So, a continuous decrease of ζ was observed at c TA > 4 mmol L−1. Similar results are also obtained in the literatures [39,40]. Finally, the ζ is close to zero, but not reduced to negative. This is mainly because the negative charge of TA is much weaker than that of anionic surfactants.

The samples with the Tyndall effect in the CDs/TA mixed solutions is indicative of the formation of supramolecular self-assemblies [41,42]. Although the appearance is transparent for the sample with c TA = 12 mmol L−1, Tyndall effect was observed (Inset photograph in Fig. S4). There is no obvious Tyndall effect in both pure CDs (0.5 mmol L−1) and TA (12 mmol L−1) solution (Fig. S5), which shows that the Tyndall effect is caused by the co-assembly of CDs and TA. TEM images reveal that there is a small amount of interconnected network (Fig. S4). Now, we focused on characterizing the morphologies of the turbid phase. For the sample with c TA = 20 mmol L−1, TEM images reveal the presence of a highly interconnected network (Fig. 3 a). From the enlarged TEM image, one can see that the CDs located at the network (Fig. S6), which proves that the CDs are involved in the formation of aggregates. When the concentration of TA is from 20 mmol L−1 to 30 mmol L−1, the skeleton of the network becomes slightly thicker (Fig. S7). In addition, spheres with small size have been detected which preferentially appear at the junctions of the network (denoted by the green circles in Fig. S7). The observation indicates that the skeletons of the networks begin to fuse when meeting each other due to the decreased ζ (see Fig. 2B), which finally drives the networks to form spheres [28]. We infer that when the ζ is reduced to a certain degree, the decrease of electrostatic repulsion makes the skeletons of the networks fuse.

In the sample with 70 mmol L−1 TA, a large number of spherical aggregates with diameters ranging from 41 to 123 nm has been noticed (Fig. 3b). It can be seen from the enlarged image that CDs inside the aggregates could be clearly seen (Fig. S8), indicating the presence of CDs in supramolecular self-assemblies. When c TA was further increased to 120 mmol L−1, vesicular structures with a clear contrast between the wall and the interior are observed from HR-TEM (Fig. 3c and Fig. S9) [38,43]. The wall of vesicles formed by nanoparticles is generally thicker, so that the inner water layer is small [14]. We speculate that the wall of the vesicles formed by CDs and TA are also relatively thick, making it difficult to observe the shallow water layer structure in HR-TEM. AFM observations indicate that the height of the sphere is about one-eighth to one-fifth of the width in dried state (Fig. 3d), which further confirms the hollow feature of the vesicles [38].

Besides imaging studies, the size distributions and the polydispersity index (PDI) of these aggregates have also been characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements. Through the DLS results (Fig. 4 ) and corresponding photos (Fig. S10) at varying time, it can be seen that the size of the aggregates and the turbidity of the sample increase with time. Most samples can reach stability after three days and can be stable for more than one week. However, the sample with c TA = 70 mmol L−1 showed partial precipitation after one week (Fig. S10 C), which is due to the agglomeration and fusion of spherical aggregates. This conclusion gains further evidence from that the size (~462 nm) measured by DLS is much larger than the size (~78 nm) measured by TEM. On the other hand, the dissociation of phenolic hydroxyl groups is less at lower c TA, and only a small amount of TA is combined with the CDs. As c TA increases, more CDs are neutralized by the dissociation of phenolic hydroxyl groups and the sample with c TA = 70 mmol L−1 show partial precipitation due to the decreased electrostatic repulsion. At higher c TA, the turbidity of the solutions decreased, forming more stable aggregates. For the sample with c TA = 120 mmol L−1, the results of DLS measurement are consistent with TEM. In reference experiments, when only TA is added to the aqueous solutions without CDs, the sample remains transparent at the same concentrations (Fig. S2) and the size is less than 10 nm measured by DLS (Fig. S11). Thus, the aggregates formation is unambiguously ascribed to the interaction between CDs and TA.

We speculate that there are both hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions between CDs and TA. In order to prove it, the size distributions and morphologies of aggregates at varying temperature and pH have been characterized. As the temperature increases, the size of the aggregates decreases due to the destruction of hydrogen bonds, which proves the existence of hydrogen bonds (Fig. S12). For the sample with c TA = 20 mmol L−1, when the temperature increases to 90 °C, the aggregates evolve from the network structures to the spherical aggregates (Fig. S13). Similarly, the spherical aggregates transfer to vesicles for the sample with c TA = 70 mmol L−1 at 90 °C (Fig. S14). It can be seen that the increase of temperature leads to the advancement of phase behavior. This is due to the fact that with the increase of temperature, the hydrogen bond is destroyed, but it is beneficial to the dissociation of phenolic hydroxyl groups [30]. Therefore, the amount of negative charge of TA increases and the interaction between CDs and TA transforms from hydrogen bonds to electrostatic interactions. For the sample with c TA = 120 mmol L−1, the structure of the vesicle is maintained at 90 °C (Fig. S15), indicating that the vesicle has good stability.

Now, we study the effect of pH on aggregation behavior. The pH of TA solution is about 3, which proves that TA has been ionized. The pH values decrease with the addition of TA (Fig. S16). Although the dissociation of hydroxyls becomes difficult with the addition of TA, the total amount of dissociation of TA increases. Therefore, the amount of negative charge of TA increases accordingly. The ionization of phenolic hydroxyl groups could be controlled by changing pH. For the sample with c TA = 20 mmol L−1, after the addition of NaOH, the turbidity of the sample increases and the size of the aggregates increases to a great extent (Fig. 5 A). This is because the addition of NaOH promotes the ionization of phenolic hydroxyl groups and makes the sample move to the turbid phase. If the pH of the solution is tuned to 1 by adding HCl, the turbid solution becomes transparent and the size of the aggregates decreases (Fig. 5A). This is because the addition of HCl inhibits the ionization of phenolic hydroxyl groups and makes the sample return to the transparent phase. For the sample with c TA = 70 mmol L−1, after the addition of HCl, the turbid solution becomes transparent and the size of the aggregates decreases (Fig. 5B), which are caused by suppression of phenolic hydroxyl groups ionization. The transparent solution became turbid again by adding NaOH (Fig. 5B). In the control experiment, both pure CDs and TA solution remain transparent at different pH (Fig. S17), which proves the existence of electrostatic interaction between CDs and TA. According to our previous report, the cytotoxicity of this CDs is very low [36]. As a natural plant polyphenols, TA has strong biological and pharmacological activities [31,32]. Therefore, the biological toxicity of the supramolecular self-assemblies formed by CDs and TA will also be very low. The prepared aggregates with temperature and pH responsiveness have great potential in biological applications.

FT-IR and UV–vis spectra were employed to study the interaction between CDs and TA at the molecular level. The FT-IR spectra of pure CDs, TA and the samples with different morphologies have been characterized (Fig. S18). It could be seen that the FT-IR characteristics of the samples with different morphologies are similar and resemble those of TA instead of CDs, which indicated that there are some TA not involved in assembly in the system. The wide peak between 3300 and 3700 cm−1 is well-known for symmetric and antisymmetric O–H stretching modes. As c TA increases, the peak of O–H shifts to a lower frequency (Fig. S18B), proving the existence of hydrogen bonding [44]. The peak at 1600 cm−1 is assigned to the overlap of CC resonance vibration in the aromatic ring of TA (Fig. S18C). With increasing c TA, the peak of CC shifts to a higher frequency, indicating that more phenolic hydroxyl groups are dissociated [45]. The dissociation of phenolic hydroxyl groups gives rise to potential electrostatic interactions between CDs and TA. Similar to the results of FT-IR spectra, the UV–vis spectra of the samples with different morphologies are similar and resemble those of TA (Fig. S19). The peaks at 375 nm corresponds to n-π* transition. When TA is assembled with CDs, the absorption spectra shift to higher wavelengths, indicating that more phenolic hydroxyl groups are dissociated [35].

Combining the results of ζ, TEM, DLS, FT-IR and UV–vis spectra, the illustration of the aggregate transition in CDs/TA binary mixtures is shown in Scheme 1 . As the concentration of TA increases, the aggregates evolve from the network structures to the spherical aggregates, and finally become the vesicles. From the results of the size distributions and morphologies of aggregates at varying temperature and pH, we speculate that the hydrogen bond and electrostatic interaction between TA and CDs are the main driving force for the formation of self-assemblies. Since the ζ of the aggregate is positively charged, the CDs are located in the periphery [36]. There may be monolayer or multilayer structures in spherical aggregates. Due to the surface free energy of CDs is high, the spherical aggregates with CDs at the shell have a high free energy. The spherical aggregates adhere to each other to diminish the free energy (Fig. 3b) [38]. In another way, to minimize the free energy of the spherical aggregates, the equilibrium spherical morphologies could favor larger aggregates to reduce the charge density [46]. Therefore, spherical aggregates transfer to vesicles with the increasing TA concentration.

The photoluminescent (PL) spectra were recorded on the aqueous solution of 0.5 mg mL−1 CDs as shown in Fig. 6 A. The optimal excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelengths were found to be 400 nm and 483 nm, respectively. The aqueous solution of the CDs shows a yellowish color in water under room light and a strong blue emission under 365 nm UV irradiation (inset of Fig. 6A). Further investigations showed a wavelength-dependent excitation behavior (Fig. 6A), which is reminiscent of the PL characteristics of many other types of CDs [47].

When TA was added to the aqueous solution of 0.5 mg mL−1 CDs, the PL intensity was greatly reduced and the emission wavelength was red-shifted (Fig. S20). Based on the above findings, we speculate that the PL quenching is mainly due to the formation of aggregates/clusters as a consequence of ion-pair formation between CDs and TA [41]. The red shift of the fluorescence with the addition of TA is due to the electrostatic interaction and the formation of TA-induced aggregates of CDs via ion-pair complexation [39], which have been observed earlier for oppositely charged CDs-surfactant pairs [48]. Variation of the maximum PL intensity excited at 400 nm as a function of c TA was shown in Fig. 6B. With increasing c TA, it was found that the PL intensity decreased and gradually stabilized.

In summary, the positively-charged CDs with peripherally substituted imidazolium cations were prepared through a one-pot pyrolysis. Due to the special molecular structure of TA, a new class of supramolecular self-assemblies through co-assembly between CDs and TA was formed. As the concentration of TA increases, the aggregates evolve from the network structures to the spherical aggregates, and finally become the vesicles. The size distributions and morphologies of aggregates at varying temperature and pH indicate that there are both hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions in the system. We believe that the potential applications of the CDs will be significantly expanded after integration into aggregates, such as a new generation of biomarkers and drug delivery vehicles.

Xiaofeng Sun studied the co-assembly of CDs and TA in aqueous solutions and wrote the original draft. Hui Wang performed most of the characterizations of the self-assemblies. Jingpeng Qi prepared the CDs. Shengju Zhou and Hongguang Li designed the project, discussed the results and edited the paper. All authors contributed to the general discussion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22002075 and No. 21875129).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supramolecular materials based on AIE luminogens (AIEgens): construction and applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Complex fluids of poly (oxyethylene) monoalkyl ether nonionic surfactants

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hyperbranched polymer vesicles: from self-assembly, characterization, mechanisms, and properties to applications

Grzelczak M, Liz-Marzan L. M, Klajn R. Stimuli-Responsive Self-Assembly of Nanoparticles. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2019; 48: 1342-1361.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colloidal capsules: nano-and microcapsules with colloidal particle shells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasmall gold nanorod vesicles with enhanced tumor accumulation and fast excretion from the body for cancer therapy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient synthesis of single gold nanoparticle hybrid amphiphilic triblock copolymers and their controlled self-assembly

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu J, Wu T, Zhang G, Liu S. Efficient Synthesis of Single Gold Nanoparticle Hybrid Amphiphilic Triblock Copolymers and Their Controlled Self-Assembly. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012; 134: 7624-7627.
